title: PT Units and Processing Lines
narrationScript: Every penetrant test is the same basic process. Clean the surface. Apply penetrant and let it dwell. Remove the excess. Develop. Inspect under the right light. What changes from shop to shop is the hardware that carries parts through those steps. In this lesson we look at the two ways that hardware shows up in the field. The first is the stationary processing line, a row of tanks and stations that handles baskets of parts all day long. The second is the portable kit, a handful of aerosol cans that brings the same chemistry to a weld on a bridge or a flange in a refinery. Start with the stationary line. Picture a fluorescent line running water washable penetrant, Type one, Method A. Parts arrive precleaned and dried, because penetrant only enters a flaw that is open, clean, and dry. The first station is the penetrant tank. Parts are dipped, or sprayed, then set on a drain rack above the tank. That drain rack matters more than it looks. It lets excess penetrant run back into the tank instead of being wasted, and the drain time is dwell time. The clock for penetrant dwell, commonly ten minutes or more under typical procedures, runs while the part drains. Next comes the wash station. For Method A this is a coarse water spray, typically held at or below forty pounds per square inch, with water commonly between fifty and one hundred degrees Fahrenheit. Over the rinse tank hangs an ultraviolet lamp, and it is not decoration. It lets the operator watch the fluorescent background wash away and stop the moment the surface is clean, because washing past that point starts pulling penetrant back out of flaws. What happens next depends on the developer. If the line uses aqueous developer, the water soluble or water suspendible kind, the wet parts go into the developer tank first and then into the dryer. If the line uses dry powder or nonaqueous developer, the parts are dried first, because those developers need a dry surface. The dryer is a recirculating hot air oven with a thermostat and a timer. Typical procedures keep part temperature at or below one hundred sixty degrees Fahrenheit, because too much heat bakes the penetrant and dims the fluorescence you are counting on. The dry developer station is usually a dust cabinet or a fluidized bed where dried parts pick up a light, even coat of powder. Then comes development dwell, commonly ten minutes or more, while the developer draws penetrant back out of any flaw the way a blotter pulls ink. The last station is the inspection booth. It is a darkened area with ultraviolet lamps delivering at least one thousand microwatts per square centimeter at the part surface, and ambient white light held to about two footcandles or less. The inspector lets their eyes adapt to the dark for several minutes before evaluating anything. On a visible dye line, the booth is replaced by a well lit inspection bench with at least one hundred footcandles of white light at the surface. Lines running post emulsifiable penetrant add stations. A lipophilic line inserts an emulsifier dip and drain between the penetrant dwell and the rinse. A hydrophilic line adds a water pre rinse, then the emulsifier dip, then the final rinse. Either way, the layout of the line follows the order of the process, and parts move in one direction only. Now the portable kit. The classic field kit is visible dye, solvent removable. Type two, Method C. It holds three aerosol cans. A solvent cleaner and remover. A red dye penetrant. A nonaqueous developer. Around the cans go lint free cloths, a brush for stubborn soil, gloves, and often a contact thermometer and a light meter to prove conditions. Add a battery powered ultraviolet lamp and a fluorescent solvent removable penetrant, and the same kit handles higher sensitivity field work in a shaded spot. Whether the hardware is a forty foot line or a shoulder bag, the stations exist to control the same variables. Time. Temperature. Coverage. Removal. Light. Learn what each station does for the process and you can run either one, and more important, you can tell when one of them is quietly going wrong.
imagePrompt: A gloved technician lifts a wire basket of machined steel parts off the drain rack of a fluorescent penetrant dip tank, with the rinse station, drying oven, and darkened inspection booth stretching down the industrial processing line behind him.

sections.heading: One Process, Two Packages
sections.body:
Penetrant testing runs the same sequence everywhere: preclean, apply penetrant and let it dwell, remove the excess, develop, inspect under controlled light, then post-clean. What changes is the hardware that carries a part through those steps. A **stationary processing line** strings the steps out as physical stations — tanks, drain racks, a dryer, a developer station, and an inspection booth — sized for baskets of production parts. A **portable kit** packs the same chemistry into aerosol cans you can carry to a weld on a tank or a flange in the field.

Most stationary lines run **Type I fluorescent** penetrant, because the throughput and sensitivity demands of production work justify a darkened booth. Most portable kits run **Type II visible red dye with solvent removal (Method C)**, because that combination needs no water supply, no dark area, and no fixed equipment.

Every station on a line exists to control process variables: time, temperature, pressure, concentration, coverage, and light. Walk a line from one end to the other and you are reading the written procedure, built in steel.

sections.caption: Station-by-station flow of a fluorescent water-washable processing line, with the aqueous-developer variation and the emulsifier insertion point noted.

sections.heading: The Front Half: Preclean Through Wash
sections.body:
Parts must reach the penetrant station **clean and dry**, so a line begins with precleaning equipment — vapor degreaser, alkaline wash tank, solvent station, or ultrasonic cleaner — followed by drying, because water or solvent sitting in a flaw blocks penetrant entry as surely as dirt does.

The **penetrant station** is typically a covered dip tank with hoist or basket handling, though spray or brush application serves odd geometry. Parts come out and sit on the **drain and dwell rack** above the tank. The rack does two jobs at once: excess penetrant drains back into the tank for reuse, and the drain time is dwell time — the clock, commonly **ten minutes or more** under typical procedures, runs while the part drains. Racks are angled so penetrant does not pool in pockets.

The **wash station** on a water-washable (Method A) line is a manual spray rinse over a catch tank: coarse droplets, typically **40 psi or less**, with water around **50–100 °F**. On fluorescent lines a **UV-A lamp hangs over the rinse station** so the operator can watch the glowing background disappear and stop the instant it is gone — washing past that point strips penetrant out of flaws. Post-emulsifiable lines insert an emulsifier station (and, for hydrophilic, a pre-rinse) between the drain rack and the final wash.

sections.heading: The Back Half: Dry, Develop, Inspect
sections.body:
The **dryer** is a recirculating hot-air oven with a thermostat and a timer. Typical procedures cap **part temperature at about 160 °F** — hotter ovens are faster, but heat degrades dye and can bake penetrant in place. Parts stay in only long enough to dry.

Developer hardware depends on the form. A **dry-powder station** is a dust cabinet or fluidized bed with extraction, where dried parts pick up a thin, even powder coat. **Nonaqueous developer** is sprayed at a ventilated bench for spot and small-lot work. **Aqueous developers** (water-soluble or suspendible) are dip tanks that sit **before** the dryer, because they are applied to parts still wet from the rinse. Whatever the form, development dwell — commonly **ten minutes or more** — runs before evaluation, and over-development bleeds indications wide and fuzzy.

The **inspection booth** ends a fluorescent line: a darkened enclosure with UV-A sources delivering at least **1,000 µW/cm² at the part surface**, ambient white light held to about **2 fc** or less, and several minutes allowed for dark adaptation. A visible-dye line ends instead at a well-lit bench with at least **100 fc** of white light at the surface. A post-clean station — rinse or solvent wipe — finishes the job so residues do not corrode parts or foul later processing.

sections.title: The developer decides the station order
sections.body: **Aqueous developers (forms b/c)** are applied to **wet** parts straight from the final rinse — the dryer comes **after** the developer tank. **Dry powder (form a)** and **nonaqueous wet (forms d/e)** require a **dry** surface — the dryer comes **before** the developer station. Set a basket down at the wrong station and you get water-streaked powder or a sagging, blotchy coating — and indications you cannot trust.

sections.caption: Stations of a typical Type I, Method A processing line (typical procedure values — your written procedure governs).
rows: Remove soils, coatings, and moisture so flaws are open and dry
Cleaning per procedure; verify the surface is dry
Apply penetrant; drain returns excess; dwell clock runs
Dwell commonly 10 min or more; 40–125 °F band
Remove surface penetrant without emptying the flaws
Coarse spray, 40 psi or less, 50–100 °F; UV-A monitor
Dry parts before dry or nonaqueous developer
Part temperature about 160 °F max; minimum time
Blot penetrant back out and spread it for visibility
Thin, even coat; development commonly 10 min or more
UV-A 1,000 µW/cm² min; ambient 2 fc max; dark adaptation

sections.heading: The Portable Kit
sections.body:
The classic field kit is **Type II visible dye, Method C (solvent-removable)** in three aerosol cans: **solvent cleaner/remover**, **red penetrant**, and **nonaqueous developer**. Around the cans: **lint-free cloths** for removal — the rule at the part is wipe with a clean dry cloth, then a solvent-**dampened** cloth, never a flooded surface — plus a brush for stubborn soil, gloves, a contact thermometer to confirm the **40–125 °F** band, and a light meter to prove **100 fc** at the surface.

Swap the red can for a **fluorescent Method C penetrant**, add a **battery-powered UV-A lamp** and a dark viewing hood or shaded area, and the same kit does higher-sensitivity field work. Aerosol kits suit welds, machined details, and spot checks during outages — anywhere a fixed line cannot go. Their limits are real, though: can-by-can application is slow and expensive per part, removal control depends entirely on the inspector's wipe technique, and there are no tanks, gauges, or timers regulating the process for you. The kit trades the line's built-in process control for portability — which makes the technician the process control.

sections.caption: Contents of a portable Type II, Method C kit, with the fluorescent field option noted.

checkQuestions.question: A fluorescent, water-washable line uses dry powder developer. After the wash station, which station order is correct?
options: Dry developer cabinet, then dryer, then inspection booth
Dryer, then dry developer cabinet, then inspection booth
Inspection booth, then dryer, then dry developer cabinet
Dry developer cabinet, then inspection booth, then dryer
checkQuestions.explanation: Dry powder needs a dry surface, so washed parts go through the oven first, then get dusted with developer, take their development dwell, and finally reach the booth.

checkQuestions.question: Why is a UV-A lamp mounted over the rinse station of a fluorescent processing line?
options: It speeds up rinsing by warming the water
It pre-develops indications before the developer station
It lets the operator watch the fluorescent background wash away and stop before over-washing
It is required emergency lighting for the wash area
checkQuestions.explanation: Watching the glowing background under UV-A tells the operator exactly when surface penetrant is gone; washing past that point starts pulling penetrant out of flaws.

checkQuestions.question: The drain and dwell rack above the penetrant tank serves which two purposes?
options: It returns excess penetrant to the tank while the penetrant dwell clock runs
It cools parts down before they enter the wash station
It applies emulsifier evenly across the part surface
It dries parts before the developer station
checkQuestions.explanation: Draining recovers penetrant for reuse, and the drain time counts as dwell time — commonly ten minutes or more under typical procedures.

checkQuestions.question: Where does an aqueous (water-soluble or water-suspendible) developer station belong on the line?
options: After the dryer, because the coating needs oven heat to cure
Inside the inspection booth
Immediately after the penetrant tank
Before the dryer — it is applied to parts still wet from the final rinse
checkQuestions.explanation: Aqueous developers go onto wet parts straight from the rinse; the coated part is then dried. Dry powder and nonaqueous forms reverse this and require a dry surface first.

checkQuestions.question: Which set of aerosol cans belongs in a standard visible-dye portable kit?
options: Penetrant, lipophilic emulsifier, and dry powder developer
Penetrant, aqueous developer, and alkaline cleaner
Solvent cleaner/remover, red visible penetrant, and nonaqueous developer
Fluorescent penetrant, water rinse, and dry powder developer
checkQuestions.explanation: The Method C field kit is cleaner/remover, visible penetrant, and nonaqueous developer; emulsifiers, aqueous developers, and dust cabinets belong to fixed processing lines.

references: Practices such as ASTM E165 and ASTM E1417 (typical processing parameters)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 6 (typical procedure values for processing lines)
